speaker: interviewee
text: The American--

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- this is December 4, 1995. The survivor is Anna Goldenberg-Schwarcz. And this is tape number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: The American soldiers and the officers were very kind to us survivors. And we had asked them to be separated from the other [? ailment, ?] because we felt very uncomfortable. They intervened in our name to the higher authorities. And we were given a place on the outskirts of Salzburg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we built little prefabricated homes. Some of us lived in-- in bigger houses where the SS left-- lived before, and they fled. It was cold, Parsch, that part of Salzburg. But when we built our own little homes, the prefabricated, we called it New Palestine. Because we felt like we were in our own midst. Like, in our own country.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had an administration there. It was the first time in our life, after a few years, that we emerged in a free world. And we started to breathe a sigh of relief and look around. Where are we in this world, each one personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was wounded inside, very, very much. I was saddened by the loss of my whole family. That, I could not get over that. Salzburg is a beautiful city. It's the hometown of Mozart. They glorify the-- the music there. And it is a cultural city. Geographically in the Alps, beautifully located. As much as I saw everything with my eyes, in my heart, I had so much sadness that I could not enjoy it. But as I said before, it seems that I have a strong will of survival, and I went on and on. And we lived--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: We led, more or less, a pretty normal life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We worked, my husband and I, in HIAS. It is a Hebrew Immigration American-- forgot already-- institution to help to help emigrants. And there were many, many emigrants, because nobody wanted to stay in Salzburg. We all had an aim to emigrate into the free world. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In my personal life, something very big happened. I had my first child in Salzburg. She was born June 22, 1947. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Having a child is a very big achievement in every woman's life. And mine, more so. I named her after my mother. And I felt a little bit at ease with my very sad feelings. Because I felt that I gave birth to a child that carries my mother's name. It's like perpetuating life. It gave me a purpose. And I embraced that responsibility with full force. I often felt very sad. Because I felt that if my mother would be alive, a grandmother to live to see a grandchild, the big accomplishment in the world, in everybody's life. She had another grandmother, my husband's. Well, my mother-in-law was there. And she was very good to her. It was difficult to bring up my child. Because the sadness never left me. Never, never. But I did the best I could. [CRYING] I'm afraid that that sadness affected her life fully. [CRYING] Of course, I stopped working when she was born, and devoted my full, full attention to that child. I was with her day and night. Did everything in the world that was possible. And my husband, too, was a wonderful father. And we were most happy to-- to have a child. We lived in Salzburg. And we hoped to go to Israel. We wanted to-- at that time, Palestine. But we were too weak yet, to go to another place of war. Palestine was like a volcano still. There was no peace. There was nothing. And we knew that this is not the end. And for us to go again, to go through a war, it was almost impossible. And as-- as much as we wanted to go to Israel-- and I especially felt that there is no other place for a Jew to live but in they-- their own country. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A year later, Israel was established. It was one of the most wonderful thing for us to experience. We were rejoicing in the street, in Salzburg. And I felt that all the difficulties, all the trauma that I went through before, for that one moment, to live to see Israel independent, it was worth it. It was worth all that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I thought, in my generation, I experienced the worst. And I experienced the best. For 2,000 years, Jews prayed for that moment. And I was the one to live through it. I just regret that my family didn't, my parents, my brother. But this was something that I could not help. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then we-- we read the papers, how hard it was in Israel, how they had to fight first. First, the-- the struggle started. And we decided that we will go to America. Being that we were connected with our work, with all the immigrants that went to the United States, we also applied for a visa. And in March, in 1949, we came to the United States. Living in Austria was like a breather for us. As I said, Salzburg was a beautiful city. And after living under such terrible pressure in the war years, we were able to relax a little bit, even though the future was very uncertain. Excuse me. [DRINKS] But we knew that we are coming-- excuse me-- to a free world. And if we were able to manage, to survive such hardships, we knew that somehow we'll manage in-- in the United States too. My husband had some family in the United States. They welcomed us. But the struggle was our own. We have to adjust to an entirely new life, to a new language. For me it was easier, because I took English in gymnasium. So I-- I had quite a nice vocabulary. I was able to communicate. But everything was very different from what we were used to. Life in America was very different than the life that we had in Poland. But we took it in a stride. My husband was a big optimist. And he always felt that it will be better. If today is not so good, he hope-- he truly hoped that it will be better. For a while he didn't earn any money, because he just couldn't go to a shop and-- and work like other people did. Because he occupied a very important position in Poland and then in the Soviet Union. And it-- it was against his grain to-- to go to a shop. He-- first of all, he didn't know, even, how to-- to go about it. Then somebody suggested business.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in the zipper business. He had a shop, a zipper shop. That was not for him to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was not a person to exploit a worker. They-- or-- or make do with the-- with every little detail. So that business was not good for him. Well, after a few years, he had to give it up. In the meantime, another wonderful thing happened in our family. We had another child. My younger girl, Judi, was born in 1953. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we lived in Brooklyn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as it said, that new-- every child brings luck, she brought luck to us. Things got a lot better. My husband gave up the zipper business and went into real estate business. That was more to his liking, because he dealt with that in Poland, also. And he succeeded in the-- in the business. And we were able to better ourselves. We bought a house. We lived in our own house. And the children were-- went to school. And they did nicely in school first. And then they went to college. They associated with nice people. We belonged to Temple Beth El, in Brooklyn. It's a beautiful temple. We were active there. We were affiliated with Jewish organizations. I belong to Hadassah. We worked for the UJA, for the [? bonds. ?] We felt that being in the United States, maybe we contributed more to Israel than being in Israel. So they-- that gave us satisfaction. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then my older daughter, Nellie, was getting married. That was a wonderful milestone in our life. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And after that, Judi was getting married. That was also wonderful for us parents to experience. Sometimes we thought of our-- about our own wedding. And we compared it. And we rejoiced in every step, planning the wedding and-- and doing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we had-- Judi gave us the pleasure of having grandchildren. My granddaughter, Rachel, was born in 1977. That year, my mother-in-law died. My mother-in-law, Rachel Goldenberg, lived with us all that time. She was a very nice lady, and lived with the family. She was the matriarch of the family. And died at the ripe age. She was in her 90s. And when my Rachel was born, my Judi decided to give her a name, Rachel. Even though she thought that there is not a name after my brother, Joseph, she thought-- but that came about before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband continued to work until he retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband continued to work till he retired. He-- he was a wonderful man. And we had a very good life. We had a nice relationship. He was a good father and a good husband. And we had no problems on the road. We worked hand in hand, with whatever problem came up. And as I said, he was a big optimist. And he settled it before me. Because I was not an optimist. Maybe I was a pessimist or a realist. But being under his influence, it was a little easier for me. Now, he was quite a bit older than I am. But in our marriage, it did not matter. He was a companion. He was a wonderful husband. And it was-- my life was good with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: He passed away in 1993, of heart failure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now and-- when he-- he took sick with the-- he had angina. So he gave up the business. And in the wintertime, we came to West Palm Beach. We still kept our home in Brooklyn. But we used to come here for the winter. Now, my Judi had another child, David. So we have two grandchildren. They were the apple of our eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet your second husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my husband died here in-- in Florida, we buried him in New York. And I was just devastated. I just thought that this is the end of my life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I really did not see any future anymore. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, Sol Schwarcz was a friend of ours in-- in West Palm Beach. We learned how to play bridge. And we played together, bridge. We were friends. All of a sudden, we found out that his [? water-- ?] his wife died in 1992. And he took it very, very badly. He took that his life was over. We tried to comfort him, because my husband was still alive. And we were friends together. After my husband died, he tried to comfort me. It was comforting to know that you have friends. And my children were wonderful, even though they lost their father. And it was very difficult for them to accept that. But they stood by my side. And they knew that we will pull together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sol was determined to get married again. I wasn't ready for that. I said, we will be friends. We will remain friends. But he did not take that for an answer. And he pursued it till I decided that it was a year and a half went by. And I saw that life goes on. That nobody dies-- right-- whoever dies, nobody dies after. If I didn't die after my parents perished, and my brother, I felt that I'm not going to die again. And if I'm still alive, and there is somebody that wants to make my life complete, and he needs somebody just as much as I do, the companionship-- I felt so isolated and so alone. Even being with my children and being with my friends, I still felt very isolated and very alone. And here, I found there is somebody that wants to share his life with me. Nice man, met my standards. So I decided to get married. And we got married in 1994, in June. And we-- we live together. And it's a beautiful relationship. We have our families. He has his family. I have my family. But we share a lot. And I'm thankful to God that we found each other. It's just like God gave us another chance. First, God gave me another chance after the war, to resume my life further. And now I feel that I had another chance to go on with my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your experiences with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This photograph is my mother on the left, Neche Woszcin Trojb, with her cousin, Rachel [INAUDIBLE], probably in 1912, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as a young teenager.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it? Don't know. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my father, Jacob Trojb, in 1939, in Warkowicze, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a painting of a photograph of me, Chana Trojb, in Rovno in Gymnasium, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were 15 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 15 years old. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is Anna Goldenberg and Szloma Goldenberg in Salzburg, Austria, in 1945. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a monument erected in 1981, in New Montefiore Cemetery in Long Island. The victims of our families, perished in the Holocaust. Family Goldenberg, Trojb, and Luvshis. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is Sam and Anna Goldenberg, with our book that we published in 1988, This book is autobiographical and tells, in details, about life till the war, the war years, and after that, in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. --and me.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of David Grunspan, my grandson's bar mitzvah, in 1992, in New York. From right to left, the first one, my daughter, Nellie Hoffberg. Then it's Judi Grunspan, David's mother. Rachel Grunspan, his sister. In the back, the tall one is my son-in-law, Jack Grunspan. Then it's my husband, Sam Goldenberg, to the left. On the bottom is Anna Goldenberg. And in the middle, the little one is my grandson, David Grunspan. That was the highlight of our life, his bar mitzvah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] These pictures represent-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --the most wonderful people in the world. They are our saviors. We call them the [NON-ENGLISH], the righteous people of the world. On the left is Mother Kucerowa. She was the matriarch of the family, and encouraged her children to help save Jewish life. Next to her, on top to the right, is Antonina Shindelazova. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the bottom, on the right, is Vera Kucerowa. These two, on the right, were the ones that made the bunker for us, that shelter that we stay more than a year and a half. They provided for us the possibility to live every day, more or less, if I can say, comfort. In the middle is Mania Kucer, a daughter that, when I first came to her house, she was the one to wash my hair with kerosene and-- and brush out the lice from my hair. On the bottom on the left is Venush Kucer, a son. And with him is the other children. He was one of the most righteous people in the world that helped to survive many Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: --look at me.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken recently. I visited Czechoslovakia. And I was told that the Czech people repatriated from Ukraine to Czechoslovakia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The ones that saved my life are not alive anymore. But those are some of the people that they are there, from the family. From the right to left is a nephew of Shindelazova. His name is [? Vatslav ?] [? Vashak ?]. Next to him is his wife [? Jana, ?] In the middle is their son. Now on the left, that's Anne Goldenberg, me, that I visited them. And this is a brother, [? Tonyik ?], Shindelazova's brother. We are standing in the cemetery where all the people that saved my life are buried there. It says in Czech, this is a family, Kucer. And each one that's on the picture to the-- the previous picture is buried in that cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: --look at me.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a monument erected in Varkovychi, of the victims from the Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Here are buried 2,000 people, Jews from Varkovychi. I visited this place in 1993. And I was really impressed to see that there is a monument. Because here lie my very close family. And this is a place that I visited with my children. And it will always be in my memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in the Ukraine now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Varkovychi.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The local administration and the local people erected that. And they take care of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell us, who is your companion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sol Schwarcz is my companion and my wonderful husband. And I'm thankful to God, that he gave us another chance to have a life. We really gave each other our life back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sol, would you like to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I could do the same thing. After my wife died in 1992, I never thought I will get married. But when Anne became available, I decided-- I mean, I thought we would make-- we will have another chance, in our life. And it turned out the right way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
